walkthrough.title: Lay Out the Game Board
walkthrough.text: Let's set up Splendor: Duel. First, place the game board in the center of the table between both players. This is the five-by-five grid where gem tokens will be arranged. Make sure both players can comfortably reach the board.

walkthrough.title: Prepare the Token Bag
walkthrough.text: Find all twenty-five gem tokens and place them into the cloth bag. The tokens come in five gem colors plus gold tokens and pearl tokens. Give the bag a good shake to mix them up. These tokens are the currency you will use to buy jewel cards throughout the game.

walkthrough.title: Fill the Token Grid
walkthrough.text: Now draw twenty-five tokens from the bag one at a time and place them on the five-by-five grid on the board. Fill each space with one token, going row by row. The arrangement is random, and where each token lands will shape the strategic landscape for this particular game.

walkthrough.title: Set Up the Jewel Card Display
walkthrough.text: Find the three levels of jewel cards. Separate them by their back design into Level one, Level two, and Level three piles. Shuffle each level separately. Then lay out the cards in three rows near the board. Place the appropriate number of face-up cards in each row according to the setup rules.

walkthrough.title: Place the Noble and Royal Cards
walkthrough.text: Shuffle the noble cards and deal four face up near the jewel card display. These nobles provide prestige points when you meet their gem requirements. Also place the royal cards nearby. Royal cards offer special bonuses and can be claimed through certain achievements during the game.

walkthrough.title: Set Up Privilege Tokens
walkthrough.text: Place the privilege tokens in a shared supply between both players. You will earn privileges by taking three gems in a straight line from the board, and you can spend them to take an extra gem on a future turn. Privileges give you a tempo advantage, so keep track of them.

walkthrough.title: Determine First Player
walkthrough.text: Neither player starts with any tokens, cards, or resources. Decide who goes first by any method you prefer. The youngest player is a common choice. Once you have determined the first player, you are ready to begin. Let's learn the rules.

walkthrough.title: Game Overview
walkthrough.text: Splendor: Duel is a two-player gem-collecting engine builder. On each turn, you will take gems from the shared grid, buy jewel cards that give permanent gem bonuses and prestige points, or reserve cards for later. The twist is that there are three different ways to win, and you need to keep an eye on all of them.

walkthrough.title: Turn Options Overview
walkthrough.text: On your turn, you do exactly one of three things. You can take gems from the board, reserve a jewel card, or purchase a jewel card. That is it. One action per turn, then your opponent goes. The game moves quickly because turns are simple, but the decisions run deep.

walkthrough.title: Taking Gems from the Board
walkthrough.text: When you take gems, you select tokens from the five-by-five grid. You can take one gem of any type, two gems that are adjacent in a straight line but different colors, or three gems in a straight line. Taking three gems in a line earns you a privilege token as a bonus. After taking gems, refill the empty spaces on the board from the bag.

walkthrough.title: Special Tokens: Gold and Pearls
walkthrough.text: They can substitute for any gem color when purchasing cards. Pearl tokens are a special currency needed for certain high-value cards. Both gold and pearl tokens appear on the grid alongside regular gems, so you will need to plan your grid picks carefully to grab them when available.

walkthrough.title: Reserving a Card
walkthrough.text: Instead of taking gems, you can reserve a jewel card from the display. Take it into your hand, which keeps it safe from your opponent. You can hold a maximum of three reserved cards at a time. When you reserve a card, you also take one gold token from the board if one is available. Reserved cards can be purchased on a later turn.

walkthrough.title: Purchasing a Card
walkthrough.text: To buy a jewel card, pay the gem cost shown on the card by returning tokens to the supply. Each card you own gives you a permanent gem bonus that reduces future card costs. For example, if you own two cards with blue bonuses, every future card costs two fewer blue gems. This is the engine-building core of the game.

walkthrough.title: Crowns and Nobles
walkthrough.text: Some jewel cards have crown symbols on them. Crowns are important for one of the win conditions. When you meet a noble's gem bonus requirements, you claim that noble for additional prestige points. Nobles are claimed automatically at the end of your turn if you qualify.

walkthrough.title: Privileges
walkthrough.text: Privilege tokens are earned when you take three gems in a straight line from the board. On a future turn, you can spend a privilege to take one additional gem of your choice from the board. This gives you a significant tempo advantage, letting you grab key tokens before your opponent can reach them.

walkthrough.title: Token Limit
walkthrough.text: Each player can hold a maximum of ten tokens at any time. If taking gems would put you over the limit, you must return tokens to the supply until you are back at ten. Plan your gem collection carefully so you do not waste picks returning tokens you already collected.

walkthrough.title: Three Win Conditions
walkthrough.text: The game ends immediately when either player achieves any one of three victory conditions. First, accumulate twenty or more prestige points from cards and nobles. Second, collect ten or more crowns from card symbols. Third, earn ten or more prestige points in cards of a single gem color. The game ends the instant any condition is met, so stay alert to all three paths for both yourself and your opponent.

walkthrough.title: Welcome to Your First Game
walkthrough.text: In this practice tutorial, we will walk through the opening turns of a two-player game. You will see how to take gems efficiently, build your engine with card purchases, and keep an eye on multiple win conditions. Let's get started.

walkthrough.title: Survey the Grid
walkthrough.text: Before making any moves, take a moment to survey the five-by-five token grid. Look for clusters of colors you might need and note where the gold and pearl tokens landed. Also glance at the Level one jewel cards to see which gems you will need first. Planning your first few picks around affordable Level one cards gives you a head start.

walkthrough.title: Player One: Take Two Gems
walkthrough.text: Find two different-colored tokens that sit next to each other in a row, column, or diagonal, and pick colors that match a Level one card you want to buy. Grab both tokens and add them to your supply. The grid refills from the bag after your pick.

walkthrough.title: Player Two: Take Three Gems
walkthrough.text: Find three tokens sitting in a row on the grid. They can be any colors. Take all three and add them to your supply. Because you took three in a line, you also earn a privilege token. Nice. That privilege will let you snag an extra gem on a future turn.

walkthrough.title: Player One: Purchase a Level One Card
walkthrough.text: Find one whose cost matches your tokens. Pay the gems back to the supply and take the card. Place it in front of you. Notice the gem bonus symbol on the card. From now on, every card you buy costs one less of that color. Your engine has begun.

walkthrough.title: Why Early Cards Matter
walkthrough.text: That first card purchase might look small, but it is the foundation of your engine. Each card gives you a permanent bonus that reduces costs for future cards. Two or three Level one cards can make Level two cards almost free. Think of your early purchases as investments that pay dividends every turn for the rest of the game.

walkthrough.title: Beginner Mistake: Hoarding Gems
walkthrough.text: Player two decides to keep taking gems instead of buying cards. After a few turns, Player two has eight tokens but no cards. Meanwhile, Player one has been buying cheap cards and building permanent bonuses. Player two has a pile of one-time-use tokens, while Player one's engine is already reducing future costs. Tokens are spent once. Card bonuses last forever.

walkthrough.title: Recovery: Start Buying Cards
walkthrough.text: No problem, Player two. You have plenty of gems now, so start spending them. Buy a Level one card this turn and another one next turn. You will catch up quickly because your gem stockpile lets you purchase several cards in a row. The key lesson is to start buying cards early rather than hoarding tokens.

walkthrough.title: Beginner Mistake: Ignoring Your Opponent
walkthrough.text: Player one is so focused on their own engine that they do not notice Player two is accumulating crown cards. Player two has quietly collected seven crowns and only needs three more to win. This is the danger of tunnel vision. In Splendor: Duel, you must watch both your progress and your opponent's path to victory.

walkthrough.title: Recovery: Block the Crown Rush
walkthrough.text: Player one spots the danger just in time. They reserve a high-crown card from the display to deny it to Player two. Reserving also gives Player one a gold token, which is a nice bonus. When your opponent is close to a win condition, sometimes the best play is denial rather than building your own engine.

walkthrough.title: Using Privileges
walkthrough.text: Player two earned a privilege token earlier. Now is a great time to spend it. On their turn, Player two takes two gems as normal, then spends the privilege to grab one additional gem. That extra gem is exactly the color needed to purchase a Level two card next turn. Privileges shine when they let you complete a purchase one turn sooner than your opponent expects.

walkthrough.title: Beginner Mistake: Forgetting the Token Limit
walkthrough.text: Player one tries to take three gems but realizes they already have nine tokens. Taking three would put them at twelve, which exceeds the ten-token limit. Player one must either take fewer gems or return some after picking. This wasted flexibility could have been avoided by spending gems on cards before stockpiling more.

walkthrough.title: Recovery: Spend Before You Collect
walkthrough.text: The solution is simple. Buy a card first to spend some tokens, then collect gems on your next turn. Keeping your token count around six to eight gives you room to take two or three gems without hitting the cap. Remember, card bonuses are permanent. Tokens above the limit are wasted.

walkthrough.title: Mid-Game Check: Watch All Three Paths
walkthrough.text: Let's pause and assess. Both players should check three things. How close am I to twenty prestige? How many crowns do I have? Do I have a color concentration approaching ten points? Also check your opponent's numbers. The game can end suddenly when someone quietly crosses a threshold you were not watching.

walkthrough.title: Claiming a Noble
walkthrough.text: Player one now has the gem bonuses required by one of the face-up nobles. At the end of their turn, they claim the noble automatically. That noble adds prestige points to Player one's total. Nobles are a significant point boost, so keep an eye on their requirements as you choose which cards to buy.

walkthrough.title: Congratulations
walkthrough.text: You have practiced the core loop of Splendor: Duel. You have taken gems, built an engine, purchased cards, claimed nobles, used privileges, and learned to watch your opponent's win conditions. Remember: buy cards early, do not hoard tokens, and always track all three victory paths. You are ready to play a full game.

walkthrough.title: What to Explore Next
walkthrough.text:
Before you dive into your next game, here are three things that can help.

First, check out General Tips for strategy basics and common pitfalls.

Second, reference the Appendix for quick definitions of key terms and mechanics.

And most importantly, ask your personal GM Guide for help anytime. Just tap the Q&A tab and ask any question during your game. Have fun!

walkthrough.title: Buy Cards Early and Often
walkthrough.text: The single most important habit in Splendor: Duel is to start buying cards as soon as possible. Every card you own gives a permanent gem bonus that makes future cards cheaper. Two or three Level one cards purchased in the first few turns create a snowball effect where Level two cards become affordable without needing many tokens. Think of tokens as temporary currency and card bonuses as permanent income.

walkthrough.title: Watch All Three Win Conditions
walkthrough.text: Splendor: Duel can end by prestige, crowns, or color monopoly. Many beginners focus only on prestige and get blindsided by a crown rush or a quiet color stack. At the start of each turn, do a quick mental check. How close is my opponent to each condition? If they are at seven or eight crowns, you need to start denying crown cards even if it slows your own engine.

walkthrough.title: Use the Grid Strategically
walkthrough.text: The five-by-five grid is not just a token store. The positions of gems matter because you can only take tokens in specific patterns. Look for lines of three to earn privilege tokens. Also think about what you leave behind. If you take two gems from a line, you may be setting up your opponent to grab the third gem plus a privilege. Plan your picks with both your needs and your opponent's in mind.

walkthrough.title: Manage Your Token Count
walkthrough.text: The ten-token limit is easy to forget but painful when it bites. If you are sitting at nine tokens and want to take two, you will have to return one. This wastes a pick. The best practice is to buy a card before collecting more gems whenever possible. Keep your count around six to eight tokens so you always have room to pick up two or three.

walkthrough.title: Reserve Cards to Deny and Protect
walkthrough.text: Reserving is not just about securing a card for yourself. It is also a powerful denial tool. If your opponent needs a specific Level two card to complete a color monopoly, reserve it to pull it out of reach. You also get a gold token when you reserve, which acts as a wild gem. Use reservations defensively when your opponent is close to winning.

walkthrough.title: Timing Your Privilege Spend
walkthrough.text: Privileges are most valuable when they let you complete a card purchase one turn earlier than expected. Spending a privilege just to take a random extra gem is wasteful. Wait until you are one gem short of buying a key card, then use the privilege to close the gap. This catches your opponent off guard and accelerates your engine at a critical moment.

walkthrough.title: Plan Your Noble Path
walkthrough.text: Nobles provide a significant prestige boost and can push you over the twenty-point threshold. At the start of the game, look at which nobles are available and plan your card purchases to satisfy at least one noble's requirements. If you can route your engine through a noble's colors, you get free prestige points on top of the bonuses your cards already provide.

walkthrough.title: Adapt to Your Opponent
walkthrough.text: In a two-player game, every card your opponent buys is information about their strategy. If they are stacking blue cards, they might be going for a color monopoly. If they are grabbing crown cards, watch the crown count. The best players constantly adjust their plan based on what the opponent is doing. Flexibility beats rigidity in Splendor: Duel.

Think of cards as investments, tokens as expenses

Don't tunnel on prestige — crown rush and color monopoly can surprise you
Start denying when opponent is 2-3 steps from any win condition

summary.title: Adapt to Your Opponent

Flexibility beats rigidity — adjust plan based on opponent's moves

walkthrough.title: Crown Rush Strategy
walkthrough.text: The crown victory is the fastest path to winning if your opponent is not paying attention. Focus on buying cards with crown symbols, even if they give fewer prestige points. The beauty of the crown rush is that your opponent might be counting prestige and not notice you hit ten crowns. Target cards with two crowns first, as they are the most efficient. If your opponent starts blocking, pivot to a different win condition.

walkthrough.title: Color Monopoly Strategy
walkthrough.text: The color monopoly win requires ten or more prestige points in cards of a single gem color. This path rewards deep specialization. Pick one color early and buy every card of that color you can afford. Level two and Level three cards of your chosen color are your priority targets. The advantage of this strategy is that your opponent may not realize how close you are until it is too late, since they need to add up your color-specific points.

walkthrough.title: Prestige Point Engine
walkthrough.text: The prestige path is the most straightforward. Build a broad engine with cards from multiple colors, claim nobles for bonus prestige, and aim for twenty total. This is the default strategy most players follow, which makes it both the most predictable and the hardest to sneak past an experienced opponent. The key advantage is flexibility. You are never locked into one color or one card type.

walkthrough.title: Dual-Threat Play
walkthrough.text: The strongest position in Splendor: Duel is threatening two win conditions simultaneously. If you are at sixteen prestige and eight crowns, your opponent cannot block both paths at once. Every card they deny on one axis leaves you free to push on the other. Build toward this position by selecting cards that contribute to multiple win conditions whenever possible.

walkthrough.title: Grid Control and Denial
walkthrough.text: Advanced players think about the grid as a shared battlefield. When you take gems, consider not just what you need but what you are denying your opponent. If your opponent needs red gems and there is only one red token left on the grid, taking it forces them to wait for a refill. Similarly, avoid setting up lines of three that your opponent can grab for a free privilege token.

walkthrough.title: Tempo and Efficiency
walkthrough.text: Tempo means doing more with fewer turns. The most efficient turn in Splendor: Duel is buying a card using mostly permanent bonuses rather than tokens. When you can purchase a Level two card by paying only one or two tokens on top of your bonuses, you are playing at high tempo. Low-tempo turns, like taking one gem, should be minimized. Every turn spent just collecting gems is a turn your engine is not growing.

walkthrough.title: Noble Sequencing
walkthrough.text: Nobles provide prestige points on top of card bonuses, making them incredibly efficient. Look at the noble requirements at the start of the game and sequence your card purchases to claim a noble as quickly as possible. If two nobles share a color requirement, building that color first lets you pivot to whichever noble your opponent is not contesting. Claiming two nobles in a game is often enough to secure a prestige victory.

walkthrough.title: Reading Your Opponent
walkthrough.text: In a two-player game, information is king. Track your opponent's gem bonuses, crown count, and color concentrations. If they reserve a card, try to figure out which win condition it serves. If they are taking pearls aggressively, they are likely targeting a pearl-cost card. Use this information to decide whether to race toward your own win condition or slow down and play defensively with reservations and denial picks.

summary.title: Crown Rush Strategy
bullets: Focus on crown-symbol cards for fastest win path
Target 2-crown cards for efficiency
Opponent may not notice crown count until too late
Pivot if opponent starts blocking crown cards

summary.title: Color Monopoly Strategy
bullets: Pick one color early → buy every card of that color
Level 2-3 cards of chosen color are priority
Hard for opponent to track your color-specific point total
Deep specialization rewards commitment

summary.title: Prestige Point Engine
bullets: Build broad engine across multiple colors
Claim nobles for bonus prestige → aim for 20 total
Most predictable path but also most flexible
Default strategy — hardest to sneak past experienced opponents

summary.title: Dual-Threat Play
bullets: Threaten 2 win conditions simultaneously
Opponent can't block both paths at once
Select cards that contribute to multiple win conditions
Example: 16 prestige + 8 crowns = unblockable

summary.title: Grid Control and Denial

Avoid leaving lines of 3 for opponent's privilege tokens
Grid = shared battlefield, not just a token store

summary.title: Tempo and Efficiency

High tempo = more done in fewer turns

If 2 nobles share a color, build that color first

summary.title: Reading Your Opponent

Reserved cards reveal opponent's win condition plans

entries.definition: Victory points printed on jewel cards and noble cards. Accumulating twenty or more prestige points is one of three win conditions.
Crown symbols printed on certain jewel cards. Collecting ten or more crowns is one of three win conditions.
Accumulating ten or more prestige points in jewel cards of a single gem color. This is one of three win conditions and can end the game immediately.
Round tokens in five colors that serve as currency for purchasing jewel cards. Taken from the five-by-five grid on the game board.
A wild token that can substitute for any gem color when purchasing cards. Earned by reserving a card.
A special token required for the cost of certain high-value jewel cards. Found on the grid alongside regular gems.
Earned by taking three gems in a straight line from the grid. Spend to take one extra gem of your choice on a future turn.
Cards purchased with gem tokens that provide a permanent gem bonus, prestige points, and sometimes crowns. Come in three levels of increasing cost and value.
The cheapest jewel cards with the lowest prestige values. Best purchased early to build your engine of permanent gem bonuses.
Mid-cost jewel cards offering moderate prestige and stronger bonuses. Often affordable after acquiring a few Level one cards.
The most expensive jewel cards with the highest prestige values. Typically require a well-built engine of permanent bonuses to afford.
Cards that are automatically claimed when you meet their gem bonus requirements. Provide additional prestige points at no token cost.
Special bonus cards that can be claimed through certain achievements during the game. Provide unique advantages.
A permanent discount symbol on each jewel card. After purchasing a card, its bonus reduces the cost of all future card purchases by one gem of that color.
Taking a face-up jewel card into your hand instead of purchasing it. Maximum three reserved cards. Also grants one gold token.
Each player can hold a maximum of ten tokens. Excess tokens must be returned to the supply.
After taking gems from the five-by-five grid, empty spaces are refilled with new tokens drawn from the bag.
A horizontal, vertical, or diagonal row of adjacent spaces on the five-by-five grid. Taking three gems in a straight line earns a privilege token.
Some jewel cards have scroll abilities that grant special powers when purchased, such as taking extra gems, replaying a turn, or stealing a token.
The strategy of purchasing early cards to accumulate permanent gem bonuses that reduce costs for future, more valuable cards. The core strategic concept of Splendor: Duel.
